title: CLAUDE.md
text: S3 Object System: package uses S3 classes extensively model types (mvpa_model, rsa_model, contrast_rsa_model, etc.) generic functions (train_model, predict_model, performance). Model Registry: machine learning models stored MVPAModels environment (R/classifiers.R). Models follow standard interface: type: “Classification” “Regression” library: Required packages parameters: Tunable hyperparameters grid: Function generate parameter grids fit: Model training function predict: Prediction function prob: Probability estimation (classification) Analysis Workflows: Two main analysis approaches: Regional: Analyzes specific brain regions (ROIs) Searchlight: Sliding sphere analysis across brain Cross-validation Infrastructure: Custom cross-validation system R/crossval.R supports various schemes (k-fold, blocked, bootstrap).

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Standard Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) compares neural response patterns model‑based similarity structures. practice, may want stronger control feature space, efficient handling distance vectors block structure. vignette introduces two specialized variants rMVPA—Feature‑Based RSA Vector‑Based RSA—shows offer advantages standard RSA.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Feature‑Based RSA useful well‑defined feature space stimuli want directly map neural activity space—example, reconstructing semantic visual features brain responses. Rather correlating similarity matrices, method learns mapping neural patterns feature dimensions evaluates well features recovered.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: core distinction Feature‑Based RSA predicts feature values directly, whereas standard RSA compares similarity structures. Outputs therefore different: standard RSA produces associations RDMs, Feature‑Based RSA yields predicted feature values per stimulus. makes Feature‑Based RSA natural choice reconstruction tasks, standard RSA remains ideal testing theoretical representational models.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Let’s walk complete example:
code: ## Generate data and weakly-correlated features # Synthetic dataset: 6x6x6, 50 observations (4 blocks) data_out <- rMVPA::gen_sample_dataset(D = c(6,6,6), nobs = 50, blocks = 4, nlevels = 2) print(data_out) #> $dataset #>  #>  MVPA Dataset  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Dimensions:  6 x 6 x 6 x 50 observations  #>   - Type:  DenseNeuroVec  #> - Test Data  #>   -  None  #> - Mask Information  #>   - Areas:  TRUE : 216  #>   - Active voxels/vertices:  216  #>  #>  #> $design #>  #>  MVPA Design  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Observations:  50  #>   - Response Type:  Factor #>   - Levels:  a, b  #>   - Class Distribution:  a: 25, b: 25  #> - Test Data  #>   -  None  #> - Structure  #>   - Blocking:  Present #>   - Number of Blocks:  4  #>   - Mean Block Size:  12  (SD:  0.58 )  #>   - Split Groups:  None  set.seed(123) n_stimuli  <- 50 n_features <- 5  # Latent factors shared between features and brain data (weak signal) Z <- matrix(rnorm(n_stimuli * 2), n_stimuli, 2)  # Build a feature matrix with moderate correlation: F = 0.7 * (Z %*% B) + 0.3 * noise B <- matrix(rnorm(2 * n_features), 2, n_features) feature_matrix <- 0.7 * (Z %*% B) + 0.3 * matrix(rnorm(n_stimuli * n_features), n_stimuli, n_features) feature_matrix <- base::scale(feature_matrix) colnames(feature_matrix) <- paste0("feature_", 1:n_features)  # Create stimulus labels stim_labels <- paste0("stim_", 1:n_stimuli)  # Create a subtle latent signal in the brain data driven by the same Z mask_vol <- data_out$dataset$mask # Extract logical mask values and indices mask_vals <- as.logical(neuroim2::values(mask_vol)) mask_idx <- which(mask_vals)  # Extract masked voxel-by-time matrix from NeuroVec (V x T) vol_list <- neuroim2::vols(data_out$dataset$train_data) datamat  <- do.call(cbind, lapply(vol_list, function(v) as.numeric(v[mask_idx])))  # Two random spatial patterns within the mask set.seed(124) p1 <- rnorm(length(mask_idx)) p2 <- rnorm(length(mask_idx)) p1 <- p1 / sd(p1); p2 <- p2 / sd(p2)  # Inject moderate signal into brain data: X <- X + 0.5 * (p1 %*% t(Z1)) + 0.5 * (p2 %*% t(Z2)) datamat <- datamat + p1 %*% t(0.5 * Z[,1]) + p2 %*% t(0.5 * Z[,2])  # Repackage as a NeuroVec using the existing space and logical mask train_vec <- neuroim2::SparseNeuroVec(datamat, neuroim2::space(data_out$dataset$train_data), mask = mask_vals) dset <- mvpa_dataset(train_vec, mask = mask_vol)  # Create feature RSA design from the correlated feature matrix. # Limit max_comps so PLS and PCA use a low‑dimensional subspace, # making their behavior distinguishable in the example. feature_design <- feature_rsa_design(   F = feature_matrix,   labels = stim_labels,   max_comps = 2 )  # Create cross-validation structure using the block information crossval <- blocked_cross_validation(data_out$design$block_var)  # Create feature RSA model feature_model <- feature_rsa_model(   dataset = dset,   design = feature_design,   method = "pls",  # Partial Least Squares   crossval = crossval  # Add cross-validation )  # Create proper region mask from the dataset's mask mask_vol <- data_out$dataset$mask nvox <- sum(mask_vol) region_mask <- neuroim2::NeuroVol(   sample(1:3, size = nvox, replace = TRUE),  # 3 regions   space(mask_vol),   indices = which(mask_vol > 0) )  # Run regional analysis (suppress log messages for clarity) results <- suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(run_regional(feature_model, region_mask)))  # Examine results print(results$performance_table) #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   roinum pattern_correlation pattern_discrimination pattern_rank_percentile #>    <int>               <dbl>                  <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1      1               0.403                  0.378                   0.798 #> 2      2               0.385                  0.349                   0.786 #> 3      3               0.368                  0.335                   0.766 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: rdm_correlation <dbl>, voxel_correlation <dbl>, #> #   mse <dbl>, r_squared <dbl>, mean_voxelwise_temporal_cor <dbl>, ncomp <dbl>

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Feature‑Based RSA supports several estimators. PLS finds components maximize covariance brain data features, works well features correlated. PCA reduces feature space regression, providing compact, interpretable basis. GLMNet (elastic net) adds regularization helps feature selection multicollinearity.
code: # Compare different methods methods <- c("pls", "pca", "glmnet") results_list <- lapply(methods, function(method) {   model <- feature_rsa_model(     dataset = dset,     design = feature_design,     method = method,     crossval = crossval  # Add cross-validation   )   suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(run_regional(model, region_mask))) })  # Compare performance for (i in seq_along(methods)) {   cat("\nMethod:", methods[i], "\n")   print(results_list[[i]]$performance_table) } #>  #> Method: pls  #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   roinum pattern_correlation pattern_discrimination pattern_rank_percentile #>    <int>               <dbl>                  <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1      1               0.403                  0.378                   0.798 #> 2      2               0.385                  0.349                   0.786 #> 3      3               0.368                  0.335                   0.766 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: rdm_correlation <dbl>, voxel_correlation <dbl>, #> #   mse <dbl>, r_squared <dbl>, mean_voxelwise_temporal_cor <dbl>, ncomp <dbl> #>  #> Method: pca  #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   roinum pattern_correlation pattern_discrimination pattern_rank_percentile #>    <int>               <dbl>                  <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1      1               0.399                  0.374                   0.794 #> 2      2               0.380                  0.344                   0.779 #> 3      3               0.363                  0.330                   0.760 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: rdm_correlation <dbl>, voxel_correlation <dbl>, #> #   mse <dbl>, r_squared <dbl>, mean_voxelwise_temporal_cor <dbl>, ncomp <dbl> #>  #> Method: glmnet  #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   roinum pattern_correlation pattern_discrimination pattern_rank_percentile #>    <int>               <dbl>                  <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1      1               0.379                  0.357                   0.803 #> 2      2               0.367                  0.339                   0.787 #> 3      3               0.377                  0.351                   0.793 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: rdm_correlation <dbl>, voxel_correlation <dbl>, #> #   mse <dbl>, r_squared <dbl>, mean_voxelwise_temporal_cor <dbl>, ncomp <dbl>

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Vector‑Based RSA starts pre‑computed distance matrices operates directly vectorized distances. particularly helpful need ignore within‑block comparisons scale large datasets—avoids materializing full similarity matrices handles blocks efficiently.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Compared standard RSA, vector approach () works distance vectors instead full matrices, (ii) builds block‑wise exclusions workflow, (iii) reduces memory use retaining necessary comparisons. properties make good fit high‑resolution long‑duration datasets.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
code: # Create distance matrix for stimuli stim_distances <- as.matrix(dist(feature_matrix)) rownames(stim_distances) <- stim_labels  # Create block structure (e.g., runs) blocks <- rep(1:5, length.out = n_stimuli)  # Create vector RSA design vector_design <- vector_rsa_design(     D = stim_distances,     labels = stim_labels,     block_var = blocks )  # Create vector RSA model vector_model <- vector_rsa_model(   dataset = dset,   design = vector_design,   distfun = cordist(),  # Correlation distance   rsa_simfun = "pearson" )  # Run analysis (suppress log messages for clarity) results_vector <- suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(run_regional(vector_model, region_mask)))  # Examine results print(results_vector$performance_table) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   roinum rsa_score #>    <int>     <dbl> #> 1      1     0.549 #> 2      2     0.524 #> 3      3     0.477

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Vector-Based RSA automatically handles block structure:
code: # Compare with different block structures block_sizes <- c(5, 10) results_blocks <- lapply(block_sizes, function(size) {   blocks <- rep(1:(n_stimuli/size), each = size)   design <- vector_rsa_design(     D = stim_distances,     labels = stim_labels,     block_var = blocks   )   model <- vector_rsa_model(     dataset = dset,     design = design,     distfun = cordist()   )   suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(run_regional(model, region_mask))) })  # Compare results for (i in seq_along(block_sizes)) {   cat("\nBlock size:", block_sizes[i], "\n")   print(results_blocks[[i]]$performance_table) } #>  #> Block size: 5  #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   roinum rsa_score #>    <int>     <dbl> #> 1      1     0.552 #> 2      2     0.522 #> 3      3     0.478 #>  #> Block size: 10  #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   roinum rsa_score #>    <int>     <dbl> #> 1      1     0.554 #> 2      2     0.531 #> 3      3     0.482

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Choose Feature‑Based RSA meaningful feature space care predicting reconstructing dimensions neural activity (e.g., semantic features language regions, low‑level visual features early visual cortex).

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Use Vector‑Based RSA large datasets block handling central design. Working vectorized distances keeps memory use check makes across‑block comparisons straightforward.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: Standard RSA remains best choice primary goal test theoretical similarity structures neither strict block handling extreme memory efficiency required.

title: Advanced RSA Methods: Feature-Based and Vector-Based Approaches
text: rMVPA offers three complementary RSA workflows: standard RSA model testing, Feature‑Based RSA direct feature reconstruction, Vector‑Based RSA efficient block‑aware distance comparisons. Pick approach matches research goal, data structure, computational budget. implementation details, see reference pages feature_rsa_model(), vector_rsa_model(), rsa_model().

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: rMVPA ships two native command-line interfaces: rmvpa-searchlight voxelwise surface-neighbourhood analyses rmvpa-regional ROI/parcellation-based analyses commands resolve workflow API used inside R. share core flags data input, subsetting, model selection, cross-validation, parallel execution, output handling, configuration files. CLI flags always override values loaded YAML R config files. CLI prefers kebab-case flags --train-design; legacy underscore spellings still accepted compatibility.

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: Install package: copy packaged wrappers directory PATH: needed, add directory PATH: Confirm wrappers available: working source checkout, compatibility shims remain available: Treat packaged commands supported public interface. source checkout shims mainly useful developing inside repository.
code: pak::pak("bbuchsbaum/rMVPA") rMVPA::install_cli("~/.local/bin", overwrite = TRUE) export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" rmvpa-searchlight --help rmvpa-regional --help rmvpa-searchlight --version Rscript scripts/MVPA_Searchlight.R --help Rscript scripts/MVPA_Regional.R --help

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: used older repository-local entry points, switch packaged wrappers follows: wrapper commands call workflow API, supported interface installed-package use, documentation, long-term CLI compatibility.
code: Rscript scripts/MVPA_Searchlight.R  ->  rmvpa-searchlight Rscript scripts/MVPA_Regional.R     ->  rmvpa-regional

what: Discover the Interface from the Terminal
title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: new CLI designed can discover behaviour without leaving shell: --help shows shared command-specific options examples. --list-models prints built-model registry. Common choices include corclass (alias corsim), sda_notune, glmnet_opt, hdrda. --example-config prints minimal YAML config selected command. --dry-run validates paths, resolves defaults, builds analysis object, exits computation. searchlight analyses also reports engine eligibility. --version prints installed rMVPA version. can also register custom models R via register_mvpa_model() invoke CLI name. Domain-adaptive cross-decoding available via --model remap_rrr.
code: rmvpa-searchlight --help rmvpa-searchlight --list-models rmvpa-searchlight --example-config rmvpa-searchlight --config analysis.yaml --dry-run  rmvpa-regional --help rmvpa-regional --list-models rmvpa-regional --example-config

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: commands require: --train-design --train-data --mask Optional held-evaluation uses --test-design --test-data; supply together. surface analyses, --train-data, --test-data, --mask can take comma-separated file lists, one entry per surface section hemisphere.

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: Design files tab- space-delimited tables one row per trial. Use --label-column name target column. evaluation held-design different target field, use --test-label-column. Pass --model-type=regression label column numeric. Use --block-column=session (run) keep cross-validation splits aligned acquisition structure. --train-subset --test-subset accept R expressions evaluated design tables data loading, --split-requests grouped summaries output tables.
code: trial  condition  subject  session  run 1      Face       S01      1        1 2      House      S01      1        1 3      Face       S01      1        2 4      House      S01      1        2

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: can put settings YAML R config file override specific values command line: CLI flags override config values invocation . CLI uses kebab-case flag names, config files continue use underlying config fields pthreads, normalize_samples, class_metrics, type, niter, return_fits, pool_predictions.
code: rmvpa-searchlight \   --config searchlight.yaml \   --workers 8 \   --output sl_run_01 \   --dry-run

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: new runtime refuses write existing non-empty output directory unless opt explicitly: makes batch jobs reruns much safer: --overwrite allows existing non-empty output directory --skip--exists exits successfully without recomputing --dry-run validates inputs prints resolved configuration --print-config prints final configuration execution --preflight=warn|error|controls analysis preflight handling --save-level=minimal|standard|complete controls much written disk default standard save level, save_results() writes main outputs plus manifest. complete also writes summary tables auxiliary objects.
code: rmvpa-searchlight --config searchlight.yaml --output results rmvpa-searchlight --config searchlight.yaml --output results --overwrite rmvpa-searchlight --config searchlight.yaml --output results --skip-if-exists

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: --workers now configures temporary future plan current process instead treated passive config field. Use --future-plan=auto|sequential|multisession|multicore control strategy explicitly. --verbose enables runner logs, --seed sets RNG seed analysis built, --backend remains available advanced backend selection need override command default.

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: Feature selection specialized cross-validation schemes still best expressed config file: See vignette("FeatureSelection") vignette("CrossValidation") full option surface.
code: feature_selector:   method: anova   cutoff_type: percentile   cutoff_value: 0.1  cross_validation:   name: bootstrap   nreps: 10

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
code: rmvpa-searchlight \   --train-design train_design.tsv \   --train-data train_betas.nii.gz \   --mask brain_mask.nii.gz \   --label-column condition \   --block-column run \   --model sda_notune \   --radius 6 \   --workers 4 \   --output searchlight_results

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: rmvpa-searchlight adds flags shape local neighbourhood analysis: --radius sets searchlight radius millimetres --searchlight-type=standard|randomized|resampled chooses neighbourhood strategy --iterations sets number passes randomized resampled searchlights --engine=auto|legacy|swift|dual_lda_fast selects searchlight engine --batch-size controls many searchlights processed per batch fast validation tuning engine neighbourhood mode, use:
code: rmvpa-searchlight \   --config searchlight.yaml \   --searchlight-type randomized \   --iterations 16 \   --dry-run

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: config files, type niter underlying fields correspond CLI flags --searchlight-type --iterations.
code: train_design: train_design.tsv train_data: train_betas.nii.gz mask: brain_mask.nii.gz label_column: condition block_column: run model: sda_notune model_type: classification data_mode: image pthreads: 4 radius: 6 type: standard niter: 4 backend: auto normalize_samples: true class_metrics: true output: searchlight_results

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: searchlight run usually writes metric maps accuracy.nii.gz, auc.nii.gz, , available, prob_observed.nii.gz prob_predicted.nii.gz. class_metrics: true, per-class maps auc_class1.nii.gz also written. standard complete save levels, output directory also includes manifest describing run; complete adds auxiliary objects aux/. regression analyses, expect metrics r2.nii.gz, rmse.nii.gz, spearcor.nii.gz.

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
code: rmvpa-regional \   --train-design train_design.tsv \   --train-data train_betas.nii.gz \   --mask roi_labels.nii.gz \   --label-column condition \   --block-column run \   --model sda_notune \   --workers 4 \   --output regional_results

what: What should the mask look like?
title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: regional analysis, --mask label map parcellation non-zero integer value defines one ROI. image mode typically 3D NIfTI atlas space training data. surface mode, pass labeled mask section hemisphere; non-zero labels treated ROI IDs. Two practical rules matter: set background excluded regions 0 keep ROI labels stable across reruns region-level tables remain comparable

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: rmvpa-regional adds options ROI-level outputs pooling: --save-predictors stores fitted ROI predictors alongside result bundle --pool-predictions=none|mean|stack computes pooled ROI-level predictions --stack-folds, --stack-seed, --stack-lambda tune stacked pooling --coalesce-design-vars merges design columns prediction table makes straightforward move independent ROI scores pooled regional prediction summaries:
code: rmvpa-regional \   --config regional.yaml \   --pool-predictions stack \   --stack-folds 5 \   --stack-seed 123

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: configs, return_fits: true field corresponds directly CLI flag --save-predictors.
code: train_design: train_design.tsv train_data: train_betas.nii.gz mask: roi_labels.nii.gz label_column: condition block_column: run model: sda_notune model_type: classification data_mode: image pthreads: 4 backend: default normalize_samples: true class_metrics: true return_fits: true pool_predictions: mean output: regional_results

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: regional run writes tabular summaries performance_table.txt prediction_table.txt. pooling enabled, also get pooled_prediction_table.txt pooled_performance_table.txt. predictor fits requested, written fits/ one .rds per ROI. result includes volumetric summaries, map outputs written alongside tables, standard complete save levels add manifest.

title: Command-Line MVPA Interfaces
text: run REMAP-RRR command line, set --model=remap_rrr provide training test datasets: Useful REMAP config fields include: Outputs include standard regional summaries plus REMAP diagnostics remap_improv, delta_frob_mean, lambda_mean. See vignette("REMAP_RRR") model-level details.
code: rmvpa-regional \   --train-design perception_design.tsv \   --test-design memory_design.tsv \   --train-data perception_betas.nii.gz \   --test-data memory_betas.nii.gz \   --mask roi_labels.nii.gz \   --model remap_rrr \   --link-by image_id \   --label-column condition \   --output remap_roi link_by: image_id remap_rank: auto remap_lambda_grid: [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1] remap_leave_one_key_out: true remap_min_pairs: 5

title: Constructing Datasets for MVPA Analysis
text: easiest way get started gen_sample_dataset(), creates complete dataset design one call: dataset bundles 4-D NeuroVec (voxels × time) binary NeuroVol mask. Two pictures make shape concrete: mid-axial slice mask, trial × run grid design. Left: mid-axial slice binary mask (active voxels dark blue). Right: trial coloured run; runs split equal-length blocks trials. can also assemble pieces manually want explicit control design data frame:
code: ds <- gen_sample_dataset(D = c(6, 6, 6), nobs = 80, blocks = 4, nlevels = 2) print(ds$dataset) #>  #>  MVPA Dataset  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Dimensions:  6 x 6 x 6 x 80 observations  #>   - Type:  DenseNeuroVec  #> - Test Data  #>   -  None  #> - Mask Information  #>   - Areas:  TRUE : 216  #>   - Active voxels/vertices:  216 print(ds$design) #>  #>  MVPA Design  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Observations:  80  #>   - Response Type:  Factor #>   - Levels:  a, b  #>   - Class Distribution:  a: 40, b: 40  #> - Test Data  #>   -  None  #> - Structure  #>   - Blocking:  Present #>   - Number of Blocks:  4  #>   - Mean Block Size:  20  (SD:  0 )  #>   - Split Groups:  None dset <- mvpa_dataset(ds$dataset$train_data, mask = ds$dataset$mask)  design_df <- data.frame(   Y     = ds$design$y_train,   block = ds$design$block_var ) mvdes <- mvpa_design(design_df, y_train = ~ Y, block_var = ~ block) mvdes #>  #>  MVPA Design  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Observations:  80  #>   - Response Type:  Factor #>   - Levels:  a, b  #>   - Class Distribution:  a: 40, b: 40  #> - Test Data  #>   -  None  #> - Structure  #>   - Blocking:  Present #>   - Number of Blocks:  4  #>   - Mean Block Size:  20  (SD:  0 )  #>   - Split Groups:  None

title: Constructing Datasets for MVPA Analysis
text: examples use eval = FALSE reference file paths replace data. example assumes 4D fMRI file (“bold.nii.gz”) corresponding 3D brain mask file (“mask.nii.gz”).
code: library(neuroim2)  # Read the fMRI data as a NeuroVec object using neuroim2::read_vec train_neurovec <- neuroim2::read_vec("path/to/bold.nii.gz", mode = "normal")  # Read the brain mask and create a NeuroVol object mask_vec <- neuroim2::read_vec("path/to/mask.nii.gz", mode = "normal") mask_vol <- NeuroVol(as.array(mask_vec), NeuroSpace(dim(mask_vec), spacing = c(2, 2, 2)))  # Create the MVPA image dataset real_dataset <- mvpa_dataset(train_data = train_neurovec, mask = mask_vol)  # Display dataset details print(real_dataset)

title: Constructing Datasets for MVPA Analysis
text: example assumes cortical geometry stored file (e.g., “subject.lh.smoothwm.asc”) signal matrix CSV format (“surface_data.csv”). signal matrix dimensions corresponding number vertices number observations.
code: ## remotes::install_github("bbuchsbaum/neurosurf") library(neurosurf)  # Load the cortical geometry geom <- read_surf_geometry("path/to/subject.lh.smoothwm.asc")  # Read the surface data from a CSV file # The CSV should not have a header and have dimensions: number of vertices x number of observations data_matrix <- as.matrix(read.csv("path/to/surface_data.csv", header = FALSE))  # Verify that the number of rows in the data matches the geometry nvert <- nrow(neurosurf::vertices(geom)) if(nrow(data_matrix) != nvert) {   stop("The number of vertices in the data does not match the geometry.") }  # Create a NeuroSurfaceVector using the geometry and the data matrix real_neurosurf <- NeuroSurfaceVector(geom, 1:nvert, data_matrix)  # Create the MVPA surface dataset; if no mask is provided, one is generated automatically real_surface_dataset <- mvpa_surface_dataset(train_data = real_neurosurf, name = "lh")  # Display dataset details print(real_surface_dataset)

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: Standard Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) asks whether brain region’s representational geometry matches predicted similarity structure, typically returning single correlation value. powerful, approach tell aspects representational structure drive effect, individual voxels contribute specific theoretical dimensions. contrast_rsa_model() implements Multi‑Dimensional Signed Representational Voxel Encoding (MS‑ReVE) approach address limitations. MS‑ReVE decomposes local representational geometry user-defined contrasts—linear combinations experimental conditions capture theoretically meaningful dimensions. example, study four object categories, might define contrasts “animate vs. inanimate” “biological vs. mechanical” test whether conceptual dimensions spatially dissociable. method produces signed voxel-wise maps showing strongly voxel supports opposes contrast. Positive values indicate voxel’s pattern aligns predicted contrast direction; negative values indicate opposition. signed, contrast-specific information enables mechanistic interpretations standard RSA’s omnibus similarity measure. vignette walks compact example explains output metrics.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: start generating small dataset defining two contrasts. Contrasts column‑centered: positive values indicate conditions pull voxel patterns one direction, negatives opposite direction. Next create model specification run tiny searchlight volume, requesting default "beta_delta" metric.
code: # Generate dummy dataset (small 6x6x6 volume, 32 samples) set.seed(42) data_info <- gen_sample_dataset(D = c(6,6,6), nobs = 32, blocks = 4)  # mvpa_dataset object mvpa_dat <- data_info$dataset  # Define two contrasts across four conditions # Each row = condition, each column = contrast K <- nresponses(data_info$design) condition_labels <- levels(data_info$design$y_train)  C_mat <- matrix(0, nrow = K, ncol = 2) rownames(C_mat) <- condition_labels  # Contrast 1: conditions {1,2} vs {3,4} C_mat[1:2, 1] <- 1 C_mat[3:4, 1] <- -1  # Contrast 2: condition 1 vs condition 2 C_mat[1, 2] <- 1 C_mat[2, 2] <- -1  # Center each contrast (required for proper interpretation) C_mat <- base::scale(C_mat, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE) colnames(C_mat) <- c("AB_vs_CD", "A_vs_B")  # Visualize the contrast matrix print("Contrast Matrix (centered):") ## [1] "Contrast Matrix (centered):" print(round(C_mat, 2)) ##   AB_vs_CD A_vs_B ## a        1      1 ## b        1     -1 ## c       -1      0 ## d       -1      0 ## e        0      0 ## attr(,"scaled:center") ## [1] 0 0 ms_des <- msreve_design(data_info$design, contrast_matrix = C_mat) cv_spec <- blocked_cross_validation(data_info$design$block_var)  model_spec <- contrast_rsa_model(   dataset = mvpa_dat,   design = ms_des,   output_metric = "beta_delta",   check_collinearity = FALSE,   cv_spec = cv_spec )  # Run a very small searchlight iterator slight <- get_searchlight(model_spec$dataset, type = "standard", radius = 2) center_indices <- which(model_spec$dataset$mask > 0) iter_res <- mvpa_iterate(model_spec, slight, center_indices, analysis_type = "searchlight")  # Inspect the beta_delta metric for the first few centers preview_ids <- seq_len(min(5, length(iter_res$performance))) preview_rows <- lapply(preview_ids, function(idx) {   perf_i <- iter_res$performance[[idx]]   vals <- if (is.list(perf_i) && !is.null(perf_i$beta_delta)) {     perf_i$beta_delta   } else {     perf_i   }    if (is.null(vals)) {     return(NULL)   }   if (is.null(names(vals)) || !any(nzchar(names(vals)))) {     names(vals) <- paste0("contrast_", seq_along(vals))   }    data.frame(     center_id = iter_res$id[idx],     contrast = names(vals),     beta_delta = as.numeric(vals),     row.names = NULL   ) }) preview_rows <- Filter(Negate(is.null), preview_rows) preview_tbl <- if (length(preview_rows) == 0) data.frame() else do.call(rbind, preview_rows) preview_tbl ##    center_id            contrast   beta_delta ## 1          1 beta_delta.AB_vs_CD -0.024391747 ## 2          1   beta_delta.A_vs_B -0.026302164 ## 3          2 beta_delta.AB_vs_CD -0.261628397 ## 4          2   beta_delta.A_vs_B -0.006443043 ## 5          3 beta_delta.AB_vs_CD -0.258305514 ## 6          3   beta_delta.A_vs_B  0.206626529 ## 7          4 beta_delta.AB_vs_CD -0.091095103 ## 8          4   beta_delta.A_vs_B -0.090866802 ## 9          5 beta_delta.AB_vs_CD -0.039160407 ## 10         5   beta_delta.A_vs_B -0.336847744

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: contrast_rsa_model can return several metrics. Multiple metrics can requested returned named list searchlight location. metric combines two key quantities different ways: β_q: RSA regression coefficient showing much contrast q explains local representational dissimilarity matrix (RDM) Δ_{q,v}: projection voxel v’s pattern onto contrast q, indicating directional alignment main output options :

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: product RSA regression coefficient (βq\beta_q) voxel’s projection onto contrast (Δq,v\Delta_{q,v}). signed quantity indicates strongly voxel supports representational difference captured contrast. Positive values mean voxel pattern aligns predicted direction; negative values indicate opposite.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: regression coefficients βq\beta_q. Useful want map much contrast explains local RDM independent voxel projections.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: projection values Δq,v\Delta_{q,v} . show raw contribution voxel contrast space weighting βq\beta_q.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: single value (per voxel) measuring well voxel’s beta-weighted pattern reconstructs empirical RDM searchlight. Higher values indicate voxel individually informative multi-contrast representational structure.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: Like beta_delta using L2-normalized contribution vector. emphasizes direction contribution rather magnitude requires normalize_delta = TRUE constructing model.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: Reliability-weighted contributions ρq,vβqΔq,v\rho_{q,v} \beta_q \Delta_{q,v}. weights ρq,v\rho_{q,v} reflect stable voxel’s contributions across cross-validation folds, highlighting consistent effects.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: sum beta-weighted, normalized contributions across contrasts (∑qβqΔ̃q,v\sum_q \beta_q \tilde{\Delta}_{q,v}). “net pull” summarizes whether voxel overall favors positive negative side contrast space. Interpretation easiest contrast matrix orthonormal. Together metrics support flexible interrogation voxel region participates specified representational contrasts.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: contrast_rsa_model extends standard RSA decomposing voxel contributions along user-defined contrasts. selecting appropriate output metrics can visualize beta weights, raw contributions, reliability-weighted effects, overall reconstruction quality. combination metrics provides rich picture representational landscape revealed analysis.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: Use MS-ReVE : Theory-driven predictions representational dimensions matter (e.g., semantic features, perceptual attributes) Multiple contrasts want test simultaneously spatially dissociate Interest signed contributions: just whether region represents distinction, voxels favor direction Use standard RSA full predicted RDM specific contrast structure, want omnibus test representational similarity.

title: Contrast RSA with contrast_rsa_model
text: Contrast centering: Contrasts must column-centered (zero mean) proper interpretation Cross-validation: Required obtain unbiased condition estimates; bootstrap resampling supported Orthogonality: composite metric requires orthonormal contrasts; otherwise interpretation ambiguous Block structure: Use keep_intra_run = FALSE exclude within-run comparisons temporal autocorrelation concern

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: Cross-validation critical component evaluating performance generalizability MVPA models. rMVPA package provides several cross-validation strategies specifically designed neuroimaging data, temporal structure run/block organization must carefully considered. vignette explains cross‑validation matters MVPA, introduces schemes tailored neuroimaging data, shows implement , closes practical guidance examples.

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: Cross-validation essential neuroimaging analyses. gives us unbiased estimates well models perform shows whether brain activity patterns truly generalize new data. separating training test sets, cross-validation prevents us overfitting noise data. also maintains temporal independence sets, crucial time-series neuroimaging data.

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: rMVPA supports blocked CV (using runs folds), k‑fold CV (random partitions), bootstrap blocked CV (resampling within runs), sequential blocked CV (ordered folds within runs), two‑fold blocked CV (fast split across runs), fully custom schemes need explicit train/test indices.

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: Blocked cross-validation common approach fMRI data. respects temporal structure data using scanning runs natural validation blocks.
code: # Create a simple blocked structure: 5 runs with 20 trials each block_var <- rep(1:5, each = 20) cval <- blocked_cross_validation(block_var) print(cval) #>  #>  Blocked Cross-Validation  #>  #> - Dataset Information  #>   - Observations:  100  #>   - Number of Folds:  5  #> - Block Information  #>   - Total Blocks:  5  #>   - Mean Block Size:  20  (SD:  0 )  #>   - Block Sizes:  1: 20, 2: 20, 3: 20, 4: 20, 5: 20

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
code: # Generate example data set.seed(123) dat <- data.frame(   x1 = rnorm(100),  # 100 trials total   x2 = rnorm(100),   x3 = rnorm(100) ) y <- factor(rep(letters[1:5], length.out = 100))  # 5 conditions  # Generate cross-validation samples samples <- crossval_samples(cval, dat, y) print(samples) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   ytrain       ytest        train               test                .id   #>   <named list> <named list> <named list>        <named list>        <chr> #> 1 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>   <resample [80 x 3]> <resample [20 x 3]> 01    #> 2 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>   <resample [80 x 3]> <resample [20 x 3]> 02    #> 3 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>   <resample [80 x 3]> <resample [20 x 3]> 03    #> 4 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>   <resample [80 x 3]> <resample [20 x 3]> 04    #> 5 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>   <resample [80 x 3]> <resample [20 x 3]> 05

code: # Create weights (e.g., based on motion parameters) weights <- runif(length(block_var)) weighted_boot_cval <- bootstrap_blocked_cross_validation(   block_var,    nreps = 20,   weights = weights ) print(weighted_boot_cval) #>  #>  Bootstrap Blocked Cross-Validation  #>  #> - Configuration  #>   - Observations:  100  #>   - Bootstrap Repetitions:  20  #> - Block Information  #>   - Total Blocks:  5  #>   - Mean Block Size:  20  (SD:  0 )  #>   - Block Sizes:  1: 20, 2: 20, 3: 20, 4: 20, 5: 20  #> - Sampling Weights  #>   - Status:  Present  #>   - Range:  [0.001, 0.020]  #>   - Non-zero Weights:  100  ( 100.0% )

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: ’s complete example using blocked cross-validation SDA classifier:
code: # Setup cross-validation block_var <- rep(1:5, each = 20) cval <- blocked_cross_validation(block_var)  # Generate data set.seed(123) dat <- data.frame(matrix(rnorm(100 * 10), 100, 10)) y <- factor(rep(letters[1:5], 20))  # Generate CV samples samples <- crossval_samples(cval, dat, y)  # Train models for each fold model_fits <- samples %>%   rowwise() %>%   mutate(fit = list(sda::sda(as.matrix(as.data.frame(train)), ytrain, verbose = FALSE)))  print(model_fits) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #> # Rowwise:  #>   ytrain       ytest       train                test                 .id   fit   #>   <named list> <named lis> <named list>         <named list>         <chr> <lis> #> 1 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>  <resample [80 x 10]> <resample [20 x 10]> 01    <sda> #> 2 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>  <resample [80 x 10]> <resample [20 x 10]> 02    <sda> #> 3 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>  <resample [80 x 10]> <resample [20 x 10]> 03    <sda> #> 4 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>  <resample [80 x 10]> <resample [20 x 10]> 04    <sda> #> 5 <fct [80]>   <fct [20]>  <resample [80 x 10]> <resample [20 x 10]> 05    <sda>

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: choosing cross‑validation strategy, align scheme data structure analysis goals. Blocked CV default fMRI respects run boundaries temporal dependence. Sequential CV helpful order matters. Bootstrap variants trade additional computation stable estimates. Two‑fold CV offers quick sanity check need speed. small datasets, bootstrap blocked CV can stabilize estimates; larger datasets, simple blocked CV often suffices. Always keep train test data run separate avoid temporal leakage. Balance stability computation time: bootstrap sequential methods costlier, whereas two‑fold CV provides fast approximations early exploration.

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: Choose right cross-validation strategy neuroimaging data. Match CV approach data structure, account temporal dependencies fMRI, use bootstrap methods need stable estimates. rMVPA package supports approaches, including custom schemes specialized needs. implementation details, see blocked_cross_validation(), bootstrap_blocked_cross_validation(), custom_cross_validation().

title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: Let’s perform regional MVPA analysis using different cross-validation strategies:
code:
# Generate a sample dataset data_out <- gen_sample_dataset(D = c(6,6,6), nobs = 80, blocks = 4, nlevels = 2)  # Create a region mask mask <- data_out$dataset$mask nvox <- sum(mask) region_mask <- neuroim2::NeuroVol(   sample(1:3, size = nvox, replace = TRUE),    space(mask),    indices = which(mask > 0) )  # Create MVPA dataset dset <- mvpa_dataset(data_out$dataset$train_data, mask = data_out$dataset$mask)  # Load the classification model mod <- load_model("sda_notune") tune_grid <- data.frame(lambda = 0.01, diagonal = FALSE)  # Example 1: Using Blocked Cross-Validation blocked_cv <- blocked_cross_validation(data_out$design$block_var) mvpa_mod_blocked <- mvpa_model(   mod,    dataset = dset,    design = data_out$design,   crossval = blocked_cv,   tune_grid = tune_grid )  # Run regional analysis with blocked CV results_blocked <- run_regional(mvpa_mod_blocked, region_mask)  # Example 2: Using Bootstrap Blocked Cross-Validation bootstrap_cv <- bootstrap_blocked_cross_validation(   data_out$design$block_var,   nreps = 10 ) mvpa_mod_boot <- mvpa_model(   mod,   dataset = dset,   design = data_out$design,   crossval = bootstrap_cv,   tune_grid = tune_grid )  # Run regional analysis with bootstrap CV results_boot <- run_regional(mvpa_mod_boot, region_mask)  # Compare performance between CV strategies cat("Blocked CV Performance:
") #> Blocked CV Performance: print(results_blocked$performance_table) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   roinum Accuracy    AUC #>    <int>    <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1      1    0.562  0.191 #> 2      2    0.6    0.305 #> 3      3    0.462 -0.135 cat("
Bootstrap CV Performance:
") #>  #> Bootstrap CV Performance: print(results_boot$performance_table) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   roinum Accuracy     AUC #>    <int>    <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1      1    0.562  0.176  #> 2      2    0.575  0.281  #> 3      3    0.475 -0.0988

what: Example: Comparing CV Strategies in Regional Analysis
title: Cross-Validation Strategies in rMVPA
text: ’s detailed comparison different CV strategies: integration demonstrates different cross-validation strategies can easily incorporated broader MVPA analysis framework, allowing researchers choose appropriate validation approach specific analysis needs. details, see vignette("Regional_Analysis") vignette("Searchlight_Analysis").
code: # Create different CV schemes cv_schemes <- list(   blocked = blocked_cross_validation(data_out$design$block_var),   bootstrap = bootstrap_blocked_cross_validation(data_out$design$block_var, nreps = 10),   twofold = twofold_blocked_cross_validation(data_out$design$block_var, nreps = 5) )  # Run regional analysis with each CV scheme results <- lapply(cv_schemes, function(cv) {   mvpa_mod <- mvpa_model(     mod,     dataset = dset,     design = data_out$design,     crossval = cv,     tune_grid = tune_grid   )   run_regional(mvpa_mod, region_mask) })  # Compare performance across CV schemes performance_comparison <- lapply(names(results), function(name) {   perf <- results[[name]]$performance_table   perf$cv_scheme <- name   perf })  # Combine results all_performance <- do.call(rbind, performance_comparison) print(all_performance) #> # A tibble: 9 × 4 #>   roinum Accuracy     AUC cv_scheme #>    <int>    <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>     #> 1      1    0.562  0.191  blocked   #> 2      2    0.6    0.305  blocked   #> 3      3    0.462 -0.135  blocked   #> 4      1    0.562  0.231  bootstrap #> 5      2    0.55   0.206  bootstrap #> 6      3    0.538 -0.0400 bootstrap #> 7      1    0.562  0.114  twofold   #> 8      2    0.562  0.288  twofold   #> 9      3    0.462 -0.15   twofold

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: rMVPA supports custom analyses cases go beyond built‑MVPA RSA models. might want compute simple univariate statistics inside ROI, apply bespoke connectivity metric searchlight sphere, prototype new method without creating full S3 model. make easy, run_custom_regional() run_custom_searchlight() let apply R function ROI sphere data reusing rMVPA’s data extraction, iteration, parallelization, error handling.

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: Custom functions offer flexibility (R function ROI/sphere data), simplicity (need define full model simple summaries), easy integration metrics packages. also inherit efficient iteration via mvpa_iterate, optional parallelism (future framework), robust per‑ROI error handling prevents one failure stopping entire run.

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: function applies custom analysis function data within predefined regions interest (ROIs). Usage: custom_func Regional Analysis: custom function must accept two arguments: roi_data: matrix tibble containing data (samples x features) current ROI. roi_info: list containing information ROI, including id (ROI number mask) indices (feature indices within dataset corresponding ROI). function must return either: - named list scalar values (example, list(mean = m, sd = s)). - single-row data frame tibble column scalar value. names list elements columns become columns final output table.
code: run_custom_regional(   dataset,      # mvpa_dataset or mvpa_surface_dataset   region_mask,  # NeuroVol or NeuroSurface mask defining ROIs   custom_func,  # Your R function   ...,          # Optional args passed to mvpa_iterate   .cores = 1,   # Number of cores for parallel processing   .verbose = FALSE # Print progress messages? )

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: function applies custom analysis function data within moving searchlight spheres across brain. Usage: custom_func Searchlight Analysis: custom function must accept two arguments: sl_data: matrix tibble containing data (samples x features_in_sphere) current searchlight sphere. sl_info: list containing information sphere, including center_index (index center voxel/vertex) indices (indices features within sphere). Similar regional version, function must return either: - named list scalar values. - single-row data frame tibble scalar columns. successfully processed spheres must return set named metrics. results aggregated brain maps (NeuroVol NeuroSurface).
code: run_custom_searchlight(   dataset,      # mvpa_dataset or mvpa_surface_dataset   custom_func,  # Your R function   radius,       # Searchlight radius (mm for volume, connections for surface)   method = "standard", # "standard" or "randomized"   niter = 100,  # Iterations for "randomized" method   ...,          # Optional args passed to mvpa_iterate   .cores = 1,   # Number of cores for parallel processing   .verbose = FALSE # Print progress messages? )

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: Let’s demonstrate functions simple custom analysis: calculating mean standard deviation signal within ROI searchlight sphere.

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: First, generate sample volumetric dataset define custom function.
code: # Generate sample dataset (e.g., 10x10x10 volume, 50 observations, 2 blocks) dset_info <- gen_sample_dataset(D = c(10, 10, 10), nobs = 50, blocks = 2, nlevels=2) dataset_obj <- dset_info$dataset  # Define our custom analysis function # It calculates mean, sd, and number of features (voxels) calculate_roi_stats <- function(data, info) {   # Inputs:   # data: matrix (samples x features) for the current ROI/sphere   # info: list with id/center_index and feature indices      # Perform calculations   mean_signal <- mean(data, na.rm = TRUE)   sd_signal <- sd(data, na.rm = TRUE)   num_features <- ncol(data)      # Return results as a named list of scalars   list(     mean_signal = mean_signal,     sd_signal = sd_signal,     n_features = num_features   ) }  # Define a version that might fail for small ROIs/spheres calculate_roi_stats_robust <- function(data, info) {     if (ncol(data) < 5) {         stop("Too few features (< 5) in this region!")     }     # If enough features, proceed as normal     mean_signal <- mean(data, na.rm = TRUE)     sd_signal <- sd(data, na.rm = TRUE)     num_features <- ncol(data)     list(         mean_signal = mean_signal,         sd_signal = sd_signal,         n_features = num_features     ) }

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: Now, create region mask run run_custom_regional. Explanation: output run_custom_regional tibble. row corresponds ROI defined region_mask_vol. columns include: - id: ROI identifier (integer value mask) - metrics returned custom_func (mean_signal, sd_signal, n_features) - error: logical flag indicating custom_func errored ROI - error_message: error message error TRUE second example, calculate_roi_stats_robust encountered ROI fewer 5 voxels (potentially ROI 4), corresponding row show error = TRUE, specific error message, NA values metrics.
code: # Create a region mask with 4 ROIs covering parts of the dataset mask mask_vol <- dataset_obj$mask mask_arr <- array(0, dim(mask_vol)) active_indices <- which(mask_vol > 0) n_active <- length(active_indices)  # Assign active voxels roughly to 4 regions set.seed(456) # for reproducibility roi_assignments <- sample(1:4, size = n_active, replace = TRUE)  # Make ROI 4 potentially small to test error handling roi_assignments[sample(which(roi_assignments==4), size=round(sum(roi_assignments==4)*0.9))] <- sample(1:3, size=round(sum(roi_assignments==4)*0.9), replace=TRUE)   mask_arr[active_indices] <- roi_assignments region_mask_vol <- NeuroVol(mask_arr, space(mask_vol))  # Run the custom regional analysis (sequentially first) custom_regional_results <- run_custom_regional(   dataset = dataset_obj,   region_mask = region_mask_vol,   custom_func = calculate_roi_stats,   .cores = 1,    .verbose = FALSE )  # Print the results table print(custom_regional_results) #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>      id mean_signal sd_signal n_features error error_message #>   <int>       <dbl>     <dbl>      <int> <lgl> <chr>         #> 1     1    -0.00838     1.00         339 FALSE ~             #> 2     2    -0.0123      0.993        314 FALSE ~             #> 3     3    -0.00193     0.998        323 FALSE ~             #> 4     4     0.0232      1.02          24 FALSE ~  # Example with error handling (using the robust function and potential small ROI 4) # Suppress expected warnings from the logger about the error suppressWarnings({     custom_regional_error_results <- run_custom_regional(       dataset = dataset_obj,       region_mask = region_mask_vol,       custom_func = calculate_roi_stats_robust, # Function that might error       .cores = 1,        .verbose = FALSE     ) })  cat("\nResults with potential errors:\n") #>  #> Results with potential errors: print(custom_regional_error_results) #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>      id mean_signal sd_signal n_features error error_message #>   <int>       <dbl>     <dbl>      <int> <lgl> <chr>         #> 1     1    -0.00838     1.00         339 FALSE ~             #> 2     2    -0.0123      0.993        314 FALSE ~             #> 3     3    -0.00193     0.998        323 FALSE ~             #> 4     4     0.0232      1.02          24 FALSE ~

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: Next, run searchlight analysis using custom function. Explanation: output run_custom_searchlight searchlight_result list : - results: named list element corresponds metric returned custom_func (example, results$mean_signal, results$sd_signal) - metric element searchlight_performance object containing - $data: NeuroVol (NeuroSurface) holding metric values brain space - $summary_stats: basic statistics (mean, sd, min, max) across map values - related metadata like $metric_name, $n_nonzero - metrics: names metrics computed - n_voxels: total voxels/vertices defined mask - active_voxels: number voxels/vertices results values output maps ($data) represent result custom_func applied searchlight sphere centered voxel. “randomized” method used, map values represent average metric across spheres included voxel.
code:
# Run the custom searchlight analysis (standard method) # Use a moderate radius; set cores > 1 for parallel execution if available # Note: Running in parallel might print messages about the future plan setting. custom_searchlight_results <- run_custom_searchlight(   dataset = dataset_obj,   custom_func = calculate_roi_stats,   radius = 4, # e.g., 4mm radius   method = "standard",   .cores = 2, # Use 2 cores if available   .verbose = FALSE )  # Print the results object summary print(custom_searchlight_results) #>  #>  Searchlight Analysis Results  #>  #> - Coverage  #>   - Voxels/Vertices in Mask:  1,000  #>   - Voxels/Vertices with Results:  1,000  #> - Output Maps (Metrics)  #>   -  mean_signal  (Type:  searchlight_performance )  #>   -  sd_signal  (Type:  searchlight_performance )  #>   -  n_features  (Type:  searchlight_performance )  # Access the results for a specific metric (e.g., mean_signal) mean_signal_map_obj <- custom_searchlight_results$results$mean_signal  # The map itself is stored in the 'data' slot mean_signal_map_vol <- mean_signal_map_obj$data cat("
Class of the mean_signal map:", class(mean_signal_map_vol), "
") #>  #> Class of the mean_signal map: DenseNeuroVol cat("Dimensions of the map:", dim(mean_signal_map_vol), "
") #> Dimensions of the map: 10 10 10  # Print summary stats for the map print(mean_signal_map_obj$summary_stats) #> $mean #> [1] -0.007061025 #>  #> $sd #> [1] 0.0096284 #>  #> $min #> [1] -0.03503245 #>  #> $max #> [1] 0.03016657  # You can plot the map using neuroim2's plotting functions (example commented out) # plot(mean_signal_map_vol)

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: custom_func requirements: ensure function strictly follows input contract (roi_data/sl_data, roi_info/sl_info) returns named list single-row tibble scalar values. Inconsistent return types names across ROIs/spheres cause aggregation errors. Parallel Processing: Set .cores > 1 speed analysis. future package backend used. control, set future::plan() calling run_custom_* function (e.g., future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 4)). Error Handling: Errors occurring within custom_func specific ROI sphere caught automatically. analysis continue, affected ROI/voxel marked error flag contain NA output. Use futile.logger::flog.warn flog.error inside custom_func tryCatch blocks rMVPA detailed debugging messages. Memory: Searchlight analyses, especially large radii many randomized iterations, can memory-intensive. Monitor usage accordingly. Randomized Searchlight: using method = "randomized", ensure metric returned custom_func meaningful averaged across overlapping spheres.

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: addition fully custom ROI/sphere functions, can plug performance metrics standard mvpa_model / run_regional / run_searchlight pipeline.

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: custom performance function always receives single result object – typically classification_result – must return numeric vector scalar metrics (named return one): pass function mvpa_model() via performance argument: named entry my_perf() (accuracy, mean_pobs, max_pobs) becomes one volumetric map searchlight regional result.
code: my_perf <- function(result) {   # classification_result: result$observed, result$predicted, result$probs   acc  <- mean(result$observed == result$predicted)   pobs <- prob_observed(result)  # prob. of the true class for each trial    c(     accuracy    = acc,     mean_pobs   = mean(pobs),     max_pobs    = max(pobs)   ) } data <- gen_sample_dataset(   D = c(6, 6, 6),   nobs = 100,   response_type = "categorical",   data_mode = "image",   blocks = 4,   nlevels = 2 )  cval  <- blocked_cross_validation(data$design$block_var) mvdes <- data$design mod   <- load_model("sda_notune")  mspec <- mvpa_model(   model      = mod,   dataset    = data$dataset,   design     = mvdes,   crossval   = cval,   performance = my_perf )  res_sl <- run_searchlight(mspec, radius = 4, method = "standard") names(res_sl$results)

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: design specifies split_by, mvpa_design stores grouping split_groups, mvpa_model() automatically applies metric globally within group: Internally, rMVPA computes my_perf(result) full result split group mvdes$split_groups. Metrics group run=1 suffixed "_run1", run=2 "_run2", . test set present, grouping indices interpreted design space mapped appropriate rows result via testind.
code: design_df <- data.frame(   Y   = rep(letters[1:2], 50),   run = rep(1:5, length.out = 100) )  mvdes <- mvpa_design(   train_design = design_df,   y_train      = ~ Y,   block_var    = ~ run,   split_by     = ~ run )  mspec <- mvpa_model(   model      = mod,   dataset    = data$dataset,   design     = mvdes,   crossval   = cval,   performance = my_perf )  res_reg <- run_regional(mspec, region_mask = data$dataset$mask) head(res_reg$performance_table)

title: Running Custom Analyses with rMVPA
text: run_custom_regional run_custom_searchlight functions provide powerful mechanism extend rMVPA’s capabilities ROI/searchlight level, performance hook mvpa_model() lets swap custom scalar metrics standard MVPA workflows. Together, tools allow integrate bespoke analyses rMVPA minimal boilerplate. implementation details, see run_custom_regional(), run_custom_searchlight(), mvpa_model().

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: ERA-RSA model (era_rsa_model()) combines: Encoding–Retrieval Accuracy (ERA) — first-order similarity encoding retrieval patterns item, Representational Geometry — second-order similarity encoding retrieval representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs). vignette walks small synthetic example encoding → retrieval cross-decoding using ERA-RSA, illustrates run regional searchlight analyses. ERA-RSA designed situations : encoding phase (e.g., stimulus viewing), retrieval phase (e.g., recognition recall), item key links encoding retrieval trials.

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: start gen_sample_dataset() treat training split encoding external test split retrieval. row train_design test_design corresponds trial. item column links encoding retrieval presentations stimulus. toy data use paired row IDs item keys example one encoding one retrieval observation per item; applied analysis, column usually stimulus, image, word, event identifier design table.
code: set.seed(123)  toy <- gen_sample_dataset(   D            = c(4, 4, 4),  # modest volume for speed   nobs         = 24,   nlevels      = 3,           # 3 item categories   blocks       = 3,           # 3 runs   external_test = TRUE,       # required for encoding → retrieval   ntest_obs     = 24 )  # Add an item key column to both train and test designs item_keys <- sprintf("item_%02d", seq_len(nrow(toy$design$train_design))) toy$design$train_design$item <- factor(item_keys, levels = item_keys) toy$design$test_design$item  <- factor(item_keys, levels = item_keys)  head(toy$design$train_design) #> # A tibble: 6 x 4 #>   Y     block_var .rownum item    #>   <fct>     <int>   <int> <fct>   #> 1 b             1       1 item_01 #> 2 a             1       2 item_02 #> 3 a             1       3 item_03 #> 4 a             1       4 item_04 #> 5 c             1       5 item_05 #> 6 b             1       6 item_06

what: key_var vs y_train / y_test and phase_var
title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: mvpa_design already contains y_train / y_test, response labels trial (often category labels). ERA-RSA adds two extra notions: key_var: item key links encoding retrieval trials belonging underlying stimulus (e.g., image ID). ERA metrics era_top1_acc operate item level: retrieval trial ask whether top match among encoding trials key_var, just category. phase_var: phase label (e.g., encoding vs retrieval). external-test setup used , train split treated encoding test split retrieval, phase_var mostly placeholder keep interface consistent. becomes important single-dataset designs phases live time series. short, y_train/y_test describe shown trial (class/category), key_var defines item trial belongs ; ERA-RSA uses item keys build encoding–retrieval similarity geometry.

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: build era_rsa_model run simple regional analysis small number regions. two similarity arguments operate different levels: distfun = cordist("pearson") says build within-phase RDM. Within region, ERA-RSA first averages trials key_var, computes one encoding RDM one retrieval RDM item-level patterns. rsa_simfun = "spearman" says compare two RDMs. second-order RSA step: lower triangle encoding RDM correlated lower triangle retrieval RDM. resulting regional metric geom_cor. external model RDM example. “model” second-order comparison encoding geometry : ask whether relative distances among encoded items preserved retrieval. performance_table contains one row per region several ERA-RSA metrics. Key metrics include: era_top1_acc — top-1 encoding→retrieval accuracy item key. era_diag_mean — mean encoding–retrieval similarity diagonal. era_diag_minus_off — diagonal minus -diagonal similarity. geom_cor — second-order correlation encoding retrieval RDMs. rsa_simfun = "spearman", rank correlation item-pair dissimilarities. quantify cross-decoding performance representational geometry alignment phases.
code: # Simple 3-region mask for demonstration region_mask <- NeuroVol(   sample(1:3, size = length(toy$dataset$mask), replace = TRUE),   space(toy$dataset$mask) )  era_ms <- era_rsa_model(   dataset   = toy$dataset,   design    = toy$design,   key_var   = ~ item,          # item key linking encoding ↔ retrieval   phase_var = ~ block_var,     # parsed for interface; train/test define phases here   distfun   = cordist("pearson"), # builds encoding and retrieval RDMs   rsa_simfun = "spearman"         # compares those RDMs; output is geom_cor )  era_ms #>  #>  Model Specification  #>  #> - Summary  #>   - Name:  era_rsa_model  #>   - Primary Class:  era_rsa_model  #>   - Class Chain:  era_rsa_model, model_spec, list  #>   - Return Predictions:  FALSE  #>   - Compute Performance:  TRUE  #>   - Has Test Set:  TRUE  #>   - Basis Count:  1  #> - Parameters  #>   - key_var: ~item #>   - phase_var: ~block_var #>   - encoding_level: 1 #>   - retrieval_level: 2 #>   - distfun: cordist / pearson #>   - rsa_simfun: spearman #>   - include_diag: TRUE #>  #>  MVPA Dataset  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Dimensions:  4 x 4 x 4 x 24 observations  #>   - Type:  DenseNeuroVec  #> - Test Data  #>   - Dimensions:  4 x 4 x 4 x 24 observations  #>   - Type:  DenseNeuroVec  #> - Mask Information  #>   - Areas:  TRUE : 64  #>   - Active voxels/vertices:  64  #>  #>  #>  MVPA Design  #>  #> - Training Data  #>   - Observations:  24  #>   - Response Type:  Factor #>   - Levels:  a, b, c  #>   - Class Distribution:  a: 8, b: 8, c: 8  #> - Test Data  #>   - Observations:  24  #>   - Class Distribution:  a: 8, b: 8, c: 8  #> - Structure  #>   - Blocking:  Present #>   - Number of Blocks:  3  #>   - Mean Block Size:  8  (SD:  0 )  #>   - Split Groups:  None  era_res <- run_regional(era_ms, region_mask) era_res #>  #>  Regional Analysis Results  #>  #> - Summary  #>   - Model:  era_rsa_model  #>   - Regions with Results:  3  #>   - Metrics:  n_items, era_top1_acc, era_diag_mean, era_diag_minus_off, geom_cor, era_diag_minus_off_same_block ...  #>   - Output Maps:  n_items, era_top1_acc, era_diag_mean, era_diag_minus_off, geom_cor, era_diag_minus_off_same_block ... head(era_res$performance_table) #> # A tibble: 3 x 11 #>   roinum n_items era_top1_acc era_diag_mean era_diag_minus_off geom_cor #>    <int>   <dbl>        <dbl>         <dbl>              <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1      1      24       0.0417       -0.0153            -0.0172  -0.0539 #> 2      2      24       0.0833       -0.0437            -0.0481   0.0179 #> 3      3      24       0.0417        0.0509             0.0387   0.0304 #> # i 5 more variables: era_diag_minus_off_same_block <dbl>, #> #   era_diag_minus_off_diff_block <dbl>, era_lag_cor <dbl>, #> #   geom_cor_run_partial <dbl>, geom_cor_xrun <dbl> era_res$performance_table[   ,   c("roinum", "era_top1_acc", "era_diag_minus_off", "geom_cor") ] #> # A tibble: 3 x 4 #>   roinum era_top1_acc era_diag_minus_off geom_cor #>    <int>        <dbl>              <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1      1       0.0417            -0.0172  -0.0539 #> 2      2       0.0833            -0.0481   0.0179 #> 3      3       0.0417             0.0387   0.0304

what: 3. When do you need ERA partitioning?
title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: ERA-RSA summary gives direct item matching raw geometry correlation. era_partition_model() asks sharper follow-question: much encoding-retrieval relationship uniquely explained -item transfer, much uniquely explained preserved representational geometry nuisance structure accounted ? makes useful single cross-decoding accuracy compressed. example, two regions similar top-1 accuracy, one may carry item-specific diagonal similarity another mainly preserves relative geometry among items. Read metrics layers: naive_top1_acc item-level direct-transfer score computed encoding--retrieval similarity matrix. xdec_Accuracy trial-level direct cross-decoding metric, matched prototype scorer used naive_xdec_model(). first_order_delta_r2 unique variance explained -item transfer cross-state similarity matrix. second_order_delta_r2 geom_cor summarize whether encoding item geometry preserved retrieval. random toy dataset, values near chance near zero. real data, region high first_order_delta_r2 weak second_order_delta_r2 supports item-specific reinstatement without much global geometry preservation. reverse pattern suggests relative arrangement items preserved even direct item identification weak. era_partition_model() can also include block, category, temporal, custom nuisance regressors item_block_enc, item_block_ret, item_time_enc, item_time_ret, item_category, first_order_nuisance, second_order_nuisance. enough paired items, set include_procrustes = TRUE add leakage-free orthogonal alignment decoder. include_procrustes controls optional decoder first learns orthogonal Procrustes map encoding prototypes retrieval prototype space. fit leave-one-item-: scoring item , rotation estimated paired items, held-retrieval item matched aligned encoding prototypes. gives Procrustes metrics procrustes_top1_acc, procrustes_diag_mean, procrustes_diag_minus_off. Use want ask whether encoding retrieval occupy representational space global rotation reflection. Leave small toy examples paired items stable held-alignment; min_procrustes_train_items sets minimum.
code: partition_ms <- era_partition_model(   dataset = toy$dataset,   design  = toy$design,   key_var = ~ item,   distfun = cordist("pearson"),   rsa_simfun = "spearman",   xdec_link_by = "item",   include_procrustes = FALSE )  partition_res <- run_regional(partition_ms, region_mask) partition_cols <- c(   "roinum",   "n_items",   "naive_top1_acc",   "xdec_Accuracy",   "first_order_delta_r2",   "second_order_delta_r2",   "geom_cor" ) partition_res$performance_table[, partition_cols] #> # A tibble: 3 x 7 #>   roinum n_items naive_top1_acc xdec_Accuracy first_order_delta_r2 #>    <int>   <dbl>          <dbl>         <dbl>                <dbl> #> 1      1      24         0.0417        0.0417             0.000244 #> 2      2      24         0.0833        0.0833             0.00198  #> 3      3      24         0.0417        0.0417             0.00123  #> # i 2 more variables: second_order_delta_r2 <dbl>, geom_cor <dbl>

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: can also run ERA-RSA searchlight mode obtain whole-brain maps metrics. searchlight_result contains: metrics: names output maps (e.g., geom_cor, era_top1_acc), results: list NeuroVol maps, one per metric. can save searchlight maps using save_results(): create one NIfTI file per metric (e.g., geom_cor.nii.gz, era_top1_acc.nii.gz) can viewed favorite neuroimaging software.
code: set.seed(456)  era_sl <- run_searchlight(   era_ms,   radius = 2,         # searchlight radius in voxels   method = "standard" # ERA-RSA currently uses standard searchlight )  era_sl #>  #>  Searchlight Analysis Results  #>  #> - Coverage  #>   - Voxels/Vertices in Mask:  64  #>   - Voxels/Vertices with Results:  64  #> - Output Maps (Metrics)  #>   -  n_items  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_top1_acc  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_diag_mean  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_diag_minus_off  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  geom_cor  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_diag_minus_off_same_block  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_diag_minus_off_diff_block  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  era_lag_cor  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  geom_cor_run_partial  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol )  #>   -  geom_cor_xrun  (Type:  DenseNeuroVol ) era_sl$metrics #>  [1] "n_items"                       "era_top1_acc"                  #>  [3] "era_diag_mean"                 "era_diag_minus_off"            #>  [5] "geom_cor"                      "era_diag_minus_off_same_block" #>  [7] "era_diag_minus_off_diff_block" "era_lag_cor"                   #>  [9] "geom_cor_run_partial"          "geom_cor_xrun" out_dir <- tempfile("era_rsa_sl_") dir.create(out_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)  save_results(era_sl, out_dir, level = "standard") list.files(file.path(out_dir, "maps"))

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: ERA-RSA can optionally incorporate item-level confounds lag variables. defined item level, trial level, must align levels key_var.

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: confound_rdms named list K×K matrices "dist" objects describing item--item nuisance structure (e.g., block/run/time), rows/columns correspond item keys. common pattern build run-based confounds: supplied, two additional metrics become available: geom_cor_run_partial: correlation encoding retrieval RDMs regressing enc/ret run RDMs. geom_cor_xrun: correlation encoding retrieval RDMs restricted item pairs differ encoding retrieval run.
code: items <- levels(toy$design$train_design$item)  # Example: per-item encoding run (modal run for each item) Mode <- function(x) { ux <- unique(x); ux[which.max(tabulate(match(x, ux)))] } item_run_enc <- sapply(items, function(it) {   Mode(toy$design$train_design$block_var[toy$design$train_design$item == it]) }) names(item_run_enc) <- items  # Example: per-item retrieval run (could differ from encoding) item_run_ret <- sample(item_run_enc)  # Build run confound RDMs on the item grid run_enc <- outer(item_run_enc, item_run_enc, FUN = function(a, b) as.numeric(a == b)) run_ret <- outer(item_run_ret, item_run_ret, FUN = function(a, b) as.numeric(a == b)) rownames(run_enc) <- colnames(run_enc) <- items rownames(run_ret) <- colnames(run_ret) <- items  era_ms_conf <- era_rsa_model(   dataset = toy$dataset,   design  = toy$design,   key_var = ~ item,   phase_var = ~ block_var,   confound_rdms = list(run_enc = run_enc, run_ret = run_ret),   item_run_enc  = factor(item_run_enc),   item_run_ret  = factor(item_run_ret) )

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: item_block encodes per-item block/condition, typically derived design$train_design$block_var: enables block-limited ERA contrasts: era_diag_minus_off_same_block: diagonal ERA minus mean similarity items block. era_diag_minus_off_diff_block: diagonal ERA minus mean similarity items different blocks.
code: item_block <- factor(item_run_enc, levels = sort(unique(item_run_enc)))  era_ms_block <- era_rsa_model(   dataset    = toy$dataset,   design     = toy$design,   key_var    = ~ item,   phase_var  = ~ block_var,   item_block = item_block )

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: item_lag numeric per-item lag encoding retrieval (e.g., retrieval onset – encoding onset), aligned item keys. used compute: era_lag_cor: Spearman correlation ERA diagonal item_lag, using complete cases. applied analyses, construct item_block, item_lag, item_run_enc, item_run_ret, confound_rdms experiment’s design tables following patterns , pass era_rsa_model().

title: ERA-RSA: Cross-Decoding Between Encoding and Retrieval
text: cross-decoding encoding retrieval, comparing encoding retrieval representational geometries. era_partition_model() separates direct item transfer, variance uniquely explained -item similarity, variance explained preserved second-order geometry. run_regional() ROI-based analyses, run_searchlight() whole-brain mapping. Outputs standard rMVPA result objects, tooling (save_results(), plotting, etc.) applies models naive_xdec_model() remap_rrr_model(). ERA-RSA particularly useful want go beyond simple accuracy ask similar geometry neural representations across phases, similarity varies across brain regions.

title: Feature Selection in rMVPA
text: searchlight 200 voxels 40 trials – far features observations. Without feature selection, classifiers may overfit noise, producing inflated accuracy inside training set fails generalize. Feature selection identifies discriminative voxels within cross-validation fold, keeping carry genuine signal. Neuroimaging datasets often contain far features informative signal. Feature selection reduces dimensionality, improves interpretability, can boost predictive performance—provided performed inside cross‑validation loop avoid selection bias. rMVPA, configure selection feature‑selector object. package supports ANOVA‑based F‑test (“FTest”) correlation‑adjusted t‑scores (“catscore”, via sda.ranking) rank features, together simple cutoffs: keep top k features top proportion p (e.g., p = 0.1 keeps top 10%).

title: Feature Selection in rMVPA
text: create feature selector rMVPA, use feature_selector() function. example, construct feature selector using FTest method top_k cutoff (selecting top 10 features): Similarly, can create feature selector selects proportion features using top_p option. example , select top 10% features based FTest ranking:
code:
suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(rMVPA)) # Create a feature selector using FTest with top_k cutoff (select top 10 features) fsel <- feature_selector(method = "FTest", cutoff_type = "top_k", cutoff_value = 10) fsel #> Feature Selector Object
-----------------------
Method:         FTest 
Cutoff Type:    top_k 
Cutoff Value:   10 
 # Create a feature selector using FTest with top_p cutoff (select top 10% of features) fsel <- feature_selector(method = "FTest", cutoff_type = "top_p", cutoff_value = 0.1) fsel #> Feature Selector Object
-----------------------
Method:         FTest 
Cutoff Type:    top_p 
Cutoff Value:   0.1

title: Feature Selection in rMVPA
text: select_features() function applies feature selection process given feature matrix X response variable Y. function returns logical vector TRUE selected features FALSE otherwise. example using simulated data: Now, let’s use top_p option. select proportion features. example, cutoff value 0.1, top 10% features selected:
code: # Simulate a response variable (categorical) Y <- factor(rep(letters[1:4], each = 25))  # Simulate a feature matrix with 100 samples and 100 features X <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 100), nrow = 100, ncol = 100)  # Apply feature selection using the FTest method with top_k cutoff fsel <- feature_selector(method = "FTest", cutoff_type = "top_k", cutoff_value = 10) selected_features <- select_features(fsel, X, Y)  # The number of selected features should be equal to the cutoff value (10) cat("Number of selected features (top_k):", sum(selected_features), "\n") #> Number of selected features (top_k): 10 # Apply feature selection using the FTest method with top_p cutoff (select top 10% of features) fsel <- feature_selector(method = "FTest", cutoff_type = "top_p", cutoff_value = 0.1) selected_features <- select_features(fsel, X, Y)  # Calculate the proportion of features selected the_proportion <- sum(selected_features) / ncol(X) cat("Proportion of features selected (top_p):", the_proportion, "\n") #> Proportion of features selected (top_p): 0.1

title: Feature Selection in rMVPA
text: Alternatively, can use catscore method perform feature selection. catscore method computes correlation-adjusted t-score feature. ’s example:
code: # Create a feature selector using catscore with top_k cutoff (select top 10 features) fsel <- feature_selector(method = "catscore", cutoff_type = "top_k", cutoff_value = 10)  # Simulate a response variable and feature matrix Y <- factor(rep(letters[1:3], length.out = 90)) X <- matrix(rnorm(90 * 50), nrow = 90, ncol = 50)  # Apply feature selection using catscore selected_features <- select_features(fsel, X, Y, ranking.score = "entropy")  cat("Number of features selected using catscore (top_k):", sum(selected_features), "\n") #> Number of features selected using catscore (top_k): 10

title: Feature Selection in rMVPA
text: Feature selection powerful tool reduce dimensionality high-dimensional datasets, especially neuroimaging applications. rMVPA, integration feature selection cross-validation workflows helps ensure models built unbiased, relevant subsets data. can choose different methods (FTest catscore) cutoff strategies (top_k vs top_p) based specific analysis needs.
